docs.title: What This Is
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP is an operational standard for building, deploying, and running PHP applications in production.

It is intentionally restrictive.

Its goal is not to define the best possible system, but to define a safe, boring, repeatable baseline that:

  • reduces decision fatigue
  • minimizes accidental complexity
  • survives team and context changes
  • remains understandable months or years later

If Bedrock PHP feels limiting, that is by design.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• Chapter 1: Purpose & Scope
  • Chapter 2: Adoption Model
  • Chapter 3: Core Values (Operational)
  • Chapter 4: Declared Defaults
  • Chapter 5: Deployment & Infrastructure
  • Chapter 6: Configuration & State
  • Chapter 7: Observability
  • Chapter 8: Performance & Scaling Limits
  • Chapter 9: Security Posture
  • Chapter 10: Maintenance & Decommissioning
  • Chapter 11: When to Leave Bedrock PHP
  • Chapter 12: Version Policy

docs.title: Purpose of This Document
docs.text:
This document defines the terms under which individuals, teams, and organizations may formally adopt, represent adherence to, and integrate Bedrock PHP into their projects.

Its goals are to:

  • keep Bedrock PHP publicly readable and accessible
  • provide a clear, tangible right when a license is purchased
  • distinguish between informal use and formal adoption
  • create a sustainable funding model for the maintenance of Bedrock PHP

This document is normative.

docs.title: 1.1 Reading and Informal Use
docs.text:
Anyone may, without license:

  • read Bedrock PHP documents
  • download Bedrock PHP documents
  • learn from or be inspired by Bedrock PHP
  • apply ideas privately or experimentally
  • reference Bedrock PHP casually in discussion

No permission or payment is required for these activities.

docs.title: 1.2 Formal Adoption and Representation
docs.text:
A license is required to:

  • state that a project, team, or organization adheres to Bedrock PHP
  • treat Bedrock PHP as an internal or external standard
  • pin a specific Bedrock PHP version as a baseline
  • include Bedrock PHP in onboarding, policy, or compliance material
  • represent alignment with Bedrock PHP to stakeholders, clients, or auditors

Formal adoption is an organizational act, not a personal belief.

docs.title: 2. What the License Grants
docs.text:
A valid Adoption & Representation License grants the right to:

  • formally adopt a specific Bedrock PHP release
  • state "We follow Bedrock PHP vX.Y" internally or externally
  • redistribute the licensed release internally
  • train teams against the licensed release
  • retain the licensed release for historical reference

The license does not grant:

  • certification or approval
  • endorsement by the Bedrock PHP authors
  • immunity from deviation
  • permission to misrepresent compliance

docs.title: 3. License Scope and Tiers
docs.text: Licenses are scoped by who is allowed to represent adoption.

docs.title: 3.1 Individual License
docs.text:
Grants:

  • formal adoption for personal projects
  • use in learning, teaching, or evaluation
  • reference during consulting work

Restrictions:

  • does not allow representing client or employer adherence

docs.title: 3.2 Team License
docs.text:
Grants:

  • formal adoption by a single team or product
  • internal redistribution within that team
  • inclusion in team documentation and onboarding

Restrictions:

  • does not extend to unrelated teams or products

docs.title: 3.3 Organization License
docs.text:
Grants:

  • formal adoption across an entire organization
  • internal redistribution without team-level restriction
  • inclusion in company-wide standards

Restrictions:

  • limited to a single legal entity
  • does not permit external redistribution

docs.title: 4. Versioning and Duration
docs.text:
Licenses are issued per Bedrock PHP release.

  • Each release is frozen and versioned
  • A license applies to the specific release it references
  • Organizations may continue using a licensed release indefinitely

A new release requires:

  • an explicit decision to adopt
  • a new license

Licenses do not imply:

  • automatic upgrades
  • continuous updates
  • evergreen maintenance

docs.title: 5. Deviation and Honesty
docs.text:
Licensed adopters may deviate from Bedrock PHP.

However:

  • deviations must be documented internally
  • representation must remain honest
  • partial or selective adoption must not be misrepresented as full adherence

The license grants the right to represent alignment, not perfection.

docs.title: 6. Prohibited Uses
docs.text:
Without explicit permission, license holders must not:

  • claim certification or endorsement
  • use Bedrock PHP branding to imply approval
  • resell or sublicense the standard
  • incorporate Bedrock PHP into competing standards

docs.title: 7. Enforcement Philosophy
docs.text:
This license is enforced through:

  • clarity, not surveillance
  • good faith, not audits
  • alignment, not punishment

Bedrock PHP relies on professional integrity, not legal intimidation.

docs.title: 8. Relationship to Profiles
docs.text:
This license governs adoption of the Bedrock PHP core standard.

Profiles:

  • are optional
  • are licensed separately
  • may require additional licenses for commercial use

Adopting Bedrock PHP does not require using any profile.

docs.title: Closing Statement
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP is free to read so that it can remain universal.

Licensing exists to make formal adoption explicit, durable, and sustainable.

If this license becomes a source of friction or fear, it has failed its purpose.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This document records a project’s explicit adoption of Bedrock PHP and defines the scope of its compliance.

It exists to eliminate ambiguity.

If this document does not exist, the project is not compliant.

docs.title: Project Identification
docs.text:
• Project name:
  • Repository:
  • Primary owner:
  • Secondary contact (optional):

docs.title: Bedrock PHP Adoption
docs.text:
• Adopted version: Bedrock PHP v1.0
  • Adoption date: YYYY-MM-DD

By completing this document, the project affirms that it:

  • follows all normative rules in the specified version
  • treats declared defaults as binding
  • understands and accepts the stop rules

docs.title: Known Deviations
docs.text:
List any intentional deviations from Bedrock PHP v1.0.

If there are none, state explicitly:

No known deviations at time of adoption.

For each deviation, include:

  • Chapter / Rule:
  • Description of deviation:
  • Reason:
  • Risk introduced:
  • Review date:

Example:

  • Chapter 5 — Rule 4 (Minimal Infrastructure Surface Area)
  • Using managed message queue due to external integration requirements
  • Required by upstream vendor contract
  • Added operational complexity
  • Review by 2026-01-01

docs.title: Review Cadence
docs.text:
This declaration will be reviewed:

  • [ ] quarterly
  • [ ] biannually
  • [ ] annually

Review includes: - validating continued fit - reassessing deviations - confirming ownership

docs.title: Exit Criteria Acknowledgement
docs.text:
The project acknowledges that it must exit Bedrock PHP if:

  • deviations accumulate across multiple chapters
  • core constraints block required outcomes
  • the system no longer operates within the declared defaults

Exit will be documented per Chapter 11.

docs.title: Sign-Off
docs.text:
By signing below, the owner affirms that this declaration is accurate.

  • Owner name:
  • Role:
  • Date:

Status: Template

docs.title: Profile: AWS
docs.text:
Status: Non-Core, Derivative Artifact Profile Type: Environment Profile Version: v1.0 Compatible Bedrock PHP Version(s): v1.0 Monetization: Paid Profile

This profile is a constrained interpretation of Bedrock PHP for teams operating primarily on AWS. It does not modify the Bedrock PHP standard.

docs.title: 1. Scope & Assumptions
docs.text:
This profile applies when:

  • AWS is the single primary cloud provider
  • The system is a single PHP application deployed as a monolith
  • The operating team size is small to medium (≤20 engineers)
  • The organization prefers managed services over bespoke infrastructure

This profile does not apply to:

  • multi-cloud active-active architectures
  • organizations with dedicated platform teams
  • event-driven or service-mesh-based systems

If these conditions are not met, this profile must not be used.

docs.title: 2. Unchanged Bedrock Defaults
docs.text:
The following Bedrock PHP defaults remain unchanged:

  • Monolith-first application architecture
  • One deployment path
  • Composer as the sole dependency mechanism
  • Logs-first observability
  • Vertical scaling before horizontal scaling
  • Explicit configuration and centralized secrets
  • Conservative dependency and upgrade posture

docs.title: 3. Strengthened or Narrowed Rules
docs.text:
Under this profile:

  • We limit AWS usage to a small, fixed set of managed services.
  • We do not introduce a second compute paradigm once one is chosen.
  • We do not introduce cross-account complexity unless forced by regulation.
  • Infrastructure variation between environments is treated as a defect.

Defaults are intentionally narrower to prevent AWS-driven sprawl.

docs.title: 4. Explicit Acceptances
docs.text:
Under this profile, we explicitly accept:

  • Higher per-unit infrastructure cost in exchange for reduced operational effort
  • Vendor lock-in to AWS-managed services
  • Slower access to cutting-edge infrastructure features

These tradeoffs are considered acceptable to preserve predictability and operability.

docs.title: 5. Explicit Rejections
docs.text:
Under this profile, we explicitly reject:

  • Kubernetes (EKS). It introduces coordination and failure modes disproportionate to this scale.
  • Multi-account application architectures. They increase cognitive load and incident complexity.
  • Service meshes and custom networking overlays.
  • Mixing container-based and VM-based deployment models.

These rejections are non-negotiable within this profile.

docs.title: 6. Stop Rules
docs.text:
The following conditions define hard stops:

  • If application latency and error budgets are met, we do not add caching layers.
  • If a single managed database meets capacity targets, we do not shard or replicate manually.
  • If deployment time remains under agreed thresholds, we do not introduce parallel deployment paths.

Optimization stops when targets are met.

docs.title: 7. Applicability Exit Conditions
docs.text:
This profile no longer applies when:

  • The organization operates more than one primary cloud provider
  • The application requires multiple independent production regions
  • The engineering team exceeds 20 engineers
  • Regulatory requirements mandate strict account or network isolation

Exiting this profile is expected under these conditions and does not represent failure.

docs.title: 8. Non-Normative Notes (Optional)
docs.text:
Non-Normative:

Teams commonly overestimate the need for AWS service breadth early. This profile exists to constrain early decisions so that AWS complexity grows only when forced by reality, not by perceived best practice.

docs.title: 9. Closing Statement
docs.text:
This profile exists to reduce AWS-specific decisions under constraint.

If following this profile introduces optionality, debate, or service proliferation, the profile has failed its purpose.

docs.title: Profile: Medium Teams (6–20)
docs.text:
Status: Non-Core, Derivative Artifact Profile Type: Team Size / Organizational Scale Profile Version: v1.0 Compatible Bedrock PHP Version(s): v1.0 Monetization: Paid Profile

This profile is a constrained interpretation of Bedrock PHP for organizations operating with a medium-sized engineering team. It does not modify the Bedrock PHP standard.

docs.title: 1. Scope & Assumptions
docs.text:
This profile applies when:

  • The engineering team size is between 6 and 20 engineers
  • The system is a single primary PHP application
  • Operational responsibility is shared, but not delegated to a dedicated platform team
  • The organization values predictability over local autonomy

This profile does not apply to:

  • teams of 5 or fewer engineers
  • organizations with dedicated SRE or platform teams
  • product organizations operating multiple independent applications at equal priority

If these conditions are not met, this profile must not be used.

docs.title: 2. Unchanged Bedrock Defaults
docs.text:
The following Bedrock PHP defaults remain unchanged:

  • Monolith-first application architecture
  • Single primary deployment path
  • One primary data store
  • Composer as the only dependency mechanism
  • Logs-first observability
  • Explicit configuration and centralized secrets
  • Conservative dependency and upgrade posture

docs.title: 3. Strengthened or Narrowed Rules
docs.text:
Under this profile:

  • We limit the number of concurrently active architectural initiatives.
  • We require defaults to be documented, not tribal.
  • We do not introduce parallel ways of doing the same thing.
  • We do not allow team-level customization of infrastructure or deployment paths.

Consistency is prioritized over local optimization.

docs.title: 4. Explicit Acceptances
docs.text:
Under this profile, we explicitly accept:

  • Slower experimentation in exchange for shared understanding
  • Reduced individual autonomy to preserve system coherence
  • Occasional friction when introducing change

These tradeoffs are accepted to prevent coordination overhead from becoming systemic risk.

docs.title: 5. Explicit Rejections
docs.text:
Under this profile, we explicitly reject:

  • Multiple deployment paths for different teams or services
  • Team-specific infrastructure or runtime variants
  • Architecture decisions made to accommodate individual preferences
  • Introducing services or abstractions to reduce interpersonal coordination

These patterns scale coordination cost faster than delivery speed.

docs.title: 6. Stop Rules
docs.text:
The following conditions define hard stops:

  • If the system can be understood end-to-end by a senior engineer within a reasonable time, we do not split it.
  • If deployment and rollback are reliable, we do not add orchestration layers.
  • If operational incidents are resolvable without escalation chains, we do not add process frameworks.

Optimization stops when clarity is preserved.

docs.title: 7. Applicability Exit Conditions
docs.text:
This profile no longer applies when:

  • The engineering organization exceeds 20 engineers
  • Multiple applications require equal operational priority
  • A dedicated platform or SRE team becomes necessary

Exiting this profile reflects organizational growth, not failure.

docs.title: 8. Non-Normative Notes (Optional)
docs.text:
Non-Normative:

Medium-sized teams often experience pressure to adopt “scaled” practices prematurely. This profile exists to delay that pressure until it is unavoidable, preserving simplicity for as long as possible.

docs.title: 9. Closing Statement
docs.text:
This profile exists to reduce coordination-driven decisions under growth.

If following this profile introduces parallel systems, optionality, or team-specific divergence, the profile has failed its purpose.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: A system is not finished when it ships. It is finished when it can be safely maintained and eventually removed.

docs.title: Rule 1: Every System Has a Named Owner
Rule
docs.text: Every system, service, and recurring operational responsibility has a clearly named owner.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Ownership includes uptime, maintenance, upgrades, and deletion.
  • Owners are empowered to make changes and remove systems.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No collective or implicit ownership.
  • No systems owned by teams that no longer exist.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If no owner can be named immediately, stop changes and assign one.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Routine maintenance is planned, visible, and treated as real work.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Dependency updates are regular.
  • Backups are tested.
  • Operational debt is addressed deliberately.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No deferring maintenance indefinitely.
  • No treating maintenance as optional or filler work.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If maintenance only happens during incidents, stop feature work and rebalance priorities.

docs.title: Rule 3: Backups Are Mandatory and Verified
Rule
docs.text: All production state has automated backups that are periodically tested.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Backup scope is explicit.
  • Restore procedures are documented.
  • Restore tests are performed.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No untested backups.
  • No assumptions that backups "probably work."

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If you cannot restore confidently, stop shipping changes until you can.

docs.title: Rule 4: Decommissioning Is a First-Class Process
Rule
docs.text: Every system must have a defined decommissioning path.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Deletion is planned, not accidental.
  • Data handling decisions are explicit.
  • Infrastructure is fully removed.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No indefinitely idle systems.
  • No zombie infrastructure.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a system has no users and no plan, begin decommissioning.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Systems without active usage or ownership are considered unhealthy.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Dormant systems are reviewed.
  • Unused components are removed.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No keeping systems "just in case."
  • No indefinite pause without decision.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a system is unused, decide: delete or recommit.

docs.title: Ownership creates leverage
docs.text:
Named ownership:

  • speeds decisions
  • clarifies responsibility
  • prevents entropy

Without owners, systems decay quietly until they fail loudly.

docs.title: Maintenance debt compounds
docs.text:
Deferred maintenance:

  • increases incident frequency
  • makes upgrades scarier
  • traps teams in outdated tooling

Regular maintenance keeps systems cheap to operate.

docs.title: Backups only matter when restored
docs.text:
Backups fail for many reasons:

  • incomplete coverage
  • silent corruption
  • outdated procedures

A backup that has never been restored is a liability, not an asset.

docs.title: Deletion reduces risk
docs.text:
Removing systems:

  • shrinks attack surface
  • reduces operational burden
  • lowers cognitive load

Deletion is often the highest-leverage reliability improvement.

docs.title: Dormant systems rot
docs.text:
Unused systems:

  • miss security updates
  • lose knowledgeable owners
  • surprise teams later

Treat inactivity as a decision point, not a neutral state.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Maintenance drift often appears as:

  • "we'll deal with it later"
  • systems no one wants to touch
  • backups no one has tested

These are signals to stop adding features.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy maintenance looks like: - regular update cadence - tested restores - systems removed when obsolete

Unhealthy maintenance looks like: - ancient dependencies - fear of touching old systems - infrastructure nobody remembers adding

Status: Draft — Chapter 10

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: A standard is successful only while its constraints reduce risk and decision-making. When constraints become a liability, the standard should be exited deliberately.

docs.title: Rule 1: Exit Is a Valid Outcome
Rule
docs.text: Leaving Bedrock PHP is an acceptable and expected outcome for some systems.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Exit is not treated as failure.
  • Exit decisions are documented and intentional.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No shaming or stigma around exiting.
  • No indefinite limbo states.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If the team feels compelled to justify staying rather than leaving, reassess fit.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: If multiple core chapters require permanent deviations, Bedrock PHP is no longer a good fit.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Deviations are tracked centrally.
  • Patterns of deviation are reviewed periodically.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No normalizing constant exceptions.
  • No redefining rules informally.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If deviations span multiple chapters, begin exit planning.

docs.title: Rule 3: Certain Constraints Override Bedrock PHP
Rule
docs.text:
Some constraints supersede Bedrock PHP by necessity.

Valid examples include: - regulatory or compliance requirements - organizational scale requiring independent team autonomy - availability or latency requirements exceeding simple architectures

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Bedrock PHP does not compete with hard constraints.
  • The system evolves to meet reality.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text: • No forcing Bedrock PHP where it clearly does not fit.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If Bedrock PHP blocks required outcomes, exit.

docs.title: Rule 4: Exit Must Be Explicit and Documented
Rule
docs.text: Exiting Bedrock PHP requires a written record.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Exit date is recorded.
  • Reasons are stated.
  • Replacement approach is named.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No silent exits.
  • No continuing to claim compliance after exit.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If the system no longer follows the rules, stop using the label immediately.

docs.title: Why defining exits matters
docs.text:
Most standards fail quietly.

Teams drift away while: - still using the language - still claiming compliance - no longer following the rules

This creates confusion and false confidence.

Explicit exits preserve honesty.

docs.title: Bedrock PHP optimizes for a phase
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP is strongest when:

  • teams are small to mid-sized
  • systems are long-lived but not hyper-scale
  • operational simplicity is a competitive advantage

As systems grow: - coordination costs increase - specialization emerges - constraints change

Outgrowing the standard is healthy.

docs.title: Common exit signals
docs.text:
Signals that it may be time to leave:

  • frequent exceptions to deployment rules
  • persistent pressure for multiple runtimes or pipelines
  • operational needs that demand complex orchestration
  • repeated arguments against core defaults

These are not failures — they are signals.

docs.title: Exiting cleanly preserves trust
docs.text:
A clean exit:

  • aligns expectations
  • avoids half-adopted rules
  • clarifies new responsibilities

It is better to leave cleanly than to stay dishonestly.

docs.title: Example exit note
docs.text:
We are exiting Bedrock PHP v1.0 as of 2026-03-01. Primary drivers: regulatory requirements and multi-team autonomy. Deviations required: multiple deployment pipelines, custom auth controls. Replacement approach: internal platform standard v3.

This level of clarity is sufficient.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Unhealthy exits often look like:

  • rules quietly ignored
  • language retained without meaning
  • new complexity layered without acknowledgment

These erode trust in standards overall.

Status: Draft — Chapter 11

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: The standard itself must obey the same discipline it imposes: clarity, stability, and deliberate change.

docs.title: Rule 1: Bedrock PHP Is Versioned and Pinned
Rule
docs.text: Bedrock PHP is adopted by explicit version, not by name alone.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Projects declare a specific Bedrock PHP version (e.g., v1.0).
  • Compliance claims always include the version.
  • There is no concept of a floating or “latest” version.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No implicit upgrades.
  • No claiming compliance without a pinned version.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If the version cannot be named, stop claiming compliance.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Once released, a Bedrock PHP version is immutable.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• No silent edits.
  • No retroactive rule changes.
  • Errata require a new version.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No rewriting history.
  • No “small tweaks” without a version bump.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a change alters obligations, it requires a new release.

docs.title: Rule 3: Minor Versions Clarify, Major Versions Change Shape
Rule
docs.text:
Version numbers signal the scope of change.

  • Minor versions (v1.x) may:
  • clarify wording
  • remove ambiguity
  • tighten boundaries
  • delete content

  • add examples

  • Major versions (v2.0) may:

  • add or remove top-level chapters
  • restructure the document
  • change or remove rules

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• Minor versions must not add new obligations.
  • Major versions must not grow indefinitely.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a change increases cognitive load without removing something else, do not ship it.

docs.title: Rule 4: Every Major Version Removes Something
Rule
docs.text: Each major version must explicitly remove at least one rule, chapter, or constraint.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• The standard resists unchecked growth.
  • Complexity is pruned, not accumulated.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text: • No additive-only major releases.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If nothing can be removed, the new version is not justified.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Every change must demonstrably reduce ambiguity or decision-making.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Changelogs explain why a change exists.
  • Changes are evaluated against real confusion or failure modes.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text: • No changes for completeness, symmetry, or aesthetics alone.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a change does not make the standard easier to follow, revert it.

docs.title: Why standards rot
docs.text:
Standards decay when they:

  • grow without constraint
  • accrete edge cases
  • attempt to cover every scenario

Eventually they become unreadable and unenforceable.

Version discipline prevents this.

docs.title: Freezing versions builds trust
docs.text:
When versions are frozen:

  • teams know what they agreed to
  • upgrades can be planned
  • debates are resolved by reference

Silent changes destroy confidence.

docs.title: Removal is an act of care
docs.text:
Deleting rules:

  • forces prioritization
  • reveals what no longer matters
  • keeps the document sharp

A standard that only grows will eventually fail.

docs.title: Changelogs are part of the contract
docs.text:
A good changelog answers:

  • what changed
  • why it changed
  • what problem it solves
  • what it replaced or removed

Anything less is noise.

docs.title: Example changelog entry
docs.text:
v1.1 — Clarified deployment rollback requirements. Reduced ambiguity around acceptable rollback mechanisms. Removed non-essential example that caused confusion.

This level of detail is sufficient.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Version drift often appears as:

  • undocumented edits
  • “living documents” without releases
  • teams following different interpretations

These are signals to formalize releases.

Status: Draft — Chapter 12

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP defines a deliberately constrained way to build, deploy, and operate PHP applications in production. Its purpose is not to maximize flexibility, expressiveness, or novelty. Its purpose is to:

  • Reduce decision fatigue
  • Minimize accidental complexity
  • Produce systems that are understandable months later
  • Enable small teams to operate production systems safely

Bedrock PHP exists to answer one question consistently:

“What is the simplest thing that can reasonably work here?”

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP applies to:

  • PHP applications that serve real users
  • Systems expected to live longer than a prototype
  • Teams that want boring reliability over architectural exploration

Bedrock PHP explicitly covers:

  • deployment and infrastructure expectations
  • configuration and state handling
  • observability and operations
  • security posture
  • maintenance and end-of-life

Bedrock PHP explicitly does not attempt to define:

  • application architecture (MVC, hexagonal, DDD, etc.)
  • framework choice
  • coding style or formatting rules
  • team process, rituals, or org structure

Those choices are intentionally left outside the boundary.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
By adopting Bedrock PHP, a team agrees to:

  • accept fewer options than are technically available
  • prefer clarity over cleverness
  • remove systems instead of extending them by default

These constraints are a feature, not a limitation.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• Bedrock PHP must not be selectively adopted while ignoring its defaults.
  • Bedrock PHP must not be used as a branding label without operational compliance.
  • Bedrock PHP must not be treated as a “best practices grab bag.”

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text:
If the team finds itself repeatedly asking:

  • “Can we bend this just a little?”
  • “What if we add one more exception?”

Then stop and reassess adoption. Bedrock PHP is only useful when followed consistently.

docs.title: Why this document exists
docs.text:
Most production complexity does not come from business logic. It comes from:

  • unexamined defaults
  • ad-hoc infrastructure decisions
  • systems accreting behavior without ownership

Bedrock PHP exists to front-load those decisions so they are made once, deliberately, and then mostly forgotten.

This is not a philosophy document. It is an operational contract.

docs.title: What Bedrock PHP is good at
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP shines when:

  • the team is small (1–10 engineers)
  • the product is evolving but not experimental infrastructure-wise
  • uptime matters more than novelty
  • the cost of mistakes is higher than the cost of saying “no” to complexity

Common examples:

  • SaaS backends
  • internal tools that accidentally became critical
  • long-lived APIs
  • revenue-adjacent systems

docs.title: What Bedrock PHP is bad at
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP will feel painful if:

  • you want to explore infrastructure as a learning goal
  • the system is a research prototype
  • you need per-team autonomy across many independent services
  • you are required to meet heavy regulatory frameworks that mandate specific tooling

In these cases, the friction is a signal, not a bug.

docs.title: Things to look out for
docs.text:
Teams often violate the spirit of Bedrock PHP before they violate its rules. Watch for:

  • adding tools “just in case we need them later”
  • parallel deployment paths creeping in
  • configuration becoming implicit or tribal knowledge
  • operational work being deferred because “it works for now”

These are early warning signs of drift.

docs.title: Practical adoption advice
docs.text:
• Adopt Bedrock PHP at project start or at a clear inflection point (rewrite, ownership transfer, stability phase).
  • Write down where you knowingly diverge.
  • Revisit those divergences quarterly.

If the list grows, it may be time to exit.

Status: Draft — Chapter 1

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Adopting Bedrock PHP means explicitly committing to its constraints as a system, not as a loose set of guidelines.

Bedrock PHP is adopted at the project level, not per-feature, per-team-member, or per-phase.

docs.title: Valid adoption states
docs.text:
A project is in exactly one of the following states:

  1. Not using Bedrock PHP
  2. Using Bedrock PHP vX.Y
  3. Exiting Bedrock PHP vX.Y (temporary, transitional)

There is no partial or implicit adoption.

docs.title: Entry criteria
docs.text:
A project may adopt Bedrock PHP when:

  • there is a clear owner for the system
  • production users or downstream systems exist (or are imminent)
  • the team agrees to prioritize operational stability over architectural freedom

Adoption must be explicitly recorded (e.g. in the repository README or an architecture decision record).

docs.title: Version pinning
docs.text:
• A project must pin to a specific Bedrock PHP version.
  • Upgrading Bedrock PHP is an intentional act, not an ambient change.
  • A project may not claim compliance with multiple versions simultaneously.

docs.title: Obligations
docs.text:
By adopting Bedrock PHP, the project agrees to:

  • follow all normative rules in the selected version
  • treat declared defaults as binding unless explicitly overridden
  • document any deviations clearly and centrally

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No selective adoption (“we follow most of it”).
  • No retroactive claims of compliance.
  • No silent divergence from declared defaults.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If more than one chapter requires documented deviations to operate the system, stop claiming Bedrock PHP compliance and begin an exit process.

docs.title: Why adoption must be explicit
docs.text:
Most operational standards fail not because they are wrong, but because they are ambiguous.

When adoption is implicit:

  • teams disagree on what rules apply
  • exceptions accumulate without being named
  • standards become decorative rather than enforceable

Making adoption explicit forces a clear answer to:

“What rules are we actually operating under today?”

That clarity is more valuable than perfect rules.

docs.title: Partial adoption is worse than none
docs.text:
A common failure mode looks like this:

  • the team adopts the language of the standard
  • only the convenient parts are followed
  • the hard constraints are quietly ignored

This produces the worst outcome:

  • complexity still grows
  • confidence falsely increases
  • failures are harder to reason about because assumptions no longer match reality

If a project cannot accept a rule, it is healthier to reject the standard than to pretend.

docs.title: When to adopt Bedrock PHP
docs.text:
Good moments to adopt:

  • greenfield projects with real production intent
  • post-MVP stabilization phases
  • ownership transitions between teams
  • after a significant incident that revealed operational gaps

In these moments, constraints are usually welcomed rather than resisted.

docs.title: When not to adopt (yet)
docs.text:
Bedrock PHP may be premature when:

  • the system is a spike or proof-of-concept
  • infrastructure choices are the primary unknown being explored
  • the team expects the system to be short-lived

In these cases, defer adoption rather than dilute it.

docs.title: Versioning as a forcing function
docs.text:
Pinning a version is not bureaucracy — it is protection.

It ensures:

  • the rules you agreed to do not change underneath you
  • upgrades are deliberate and reviewable
  • disagreements can be resolved by pointing to a specific text

Treat Bedrock PHP versions like runtime versions: upgrade intentionally, not casually.

docs.title: Common adoption pitfalls
docs.text:
Watch for these early warning signs:

  • “We’ll clean this up once things settle down.”
  • “This one exception doesn’t really count.”
  • “Everyone knows how this works.”

These phrases usually precede drift.

docs.title: Practical adoption checklist
docs.text:
Before declaring adoption:

  • [ ] Name a system owner
  • [ ] Pin a Bedrock PHP version
  • [ ] Record adoption in the repo
  • [ ] Identify known deviations (if any)
  • [ ] Ensure the team understands the stop rules

If you cannot complete this checklist honestly, wait.

Status: Draft — Chapter 2

docs.title: Normative
docs.text: The following values are not aspirational. They are operational constraints. Each value exists only insofar as it changes decisions.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Prefer solutions that are widely understood, long-lived, and operationally dull over novel or elegant alternatives.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Choose tools with long track records in PHP production.
  • Prefer official, well-supported features over third-party abstractions.
  • Optimize for the median engineer, not the most senior one.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No introducing novelty primarily for interest, learning, or résumé value.
  • No replacing a working, boring system without a concrete operational reason.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If the system is stable, understandable, and meeting targets, stop changing it.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Reduce the number of valid options wherever possible.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Fewer supported ways to deploy, configure, and operate the system.
  • Clear “this is how we do it” answers beat flexible frameworks.
  • Defaults are binding unless explicitly overridden.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No maintaining multiple equivalent solutions to the same problem.
  • No deferring decisions by saying “it depends” without documenting why.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a decision has been made once and works, do not reopen it without new evidence.

docs.title: Value 3: Production Is the Only Truth
Rule
docs.text: Behavior observed in production overrides assumptions made elsewhere.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Production failures outweigh local success.
  • Operational data is treated as authoritative.
  • Debugging prioritizes production signals over reproduction attempts.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No dismissing incidents because they are “hard to reproduce.”
  • No assuming staging behavior guarantees production behavior.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If production metrics are healthy, stop optimizing based on hypothetical risks.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Every system, component, and recurring operational task has a named owner.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Ownership includes uptime, maintenance, and eventual deletion.
  • Decisions have a responsible party, not a committee.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No shared or collective ownership without accountability.
  • No “everyone knows how this works” components.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If no one can name the owner, stop expanding the system and assign ownership.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Removing systems, code paths, and infrastructure is a positive outcome.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Decommissioning is planned, not accidental.
  • Complexity reduction is valued equally to feature delivery.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No keeping unused systems “just in case.”
  • No fear-driven retention of dead code or infrastructure.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a system provides no active value, start deleting it.

docs.title: Why values are operational
docs.text:
Many teams state values like “simplicity” or “reliability” but fail to encode them into rules.

Operational values: - force tradeoffs - eliminate debate during incidents - provide justification for saying no

If a value does not change behavior under pressure, it is decorative.

docs.title: Boring systems survive people
docs.text:
Engineers leave. Context disappears. Systems remain.

Boring systems: - are easier to transfer - are easier to debug at 3am - are easier to delete safely

The goal is not to eliminate intelligence, but to avoid embedding it in fragile places.

docs.title: Constraints reduce cognitive load
docs.text:
Every additional option: - increases review time - increases onboarding cost - increases failure modes

Constraints create speed by removing the need to decide repeatedly.

Teams often mistake flexibility for empowerment; in practice, it often becomes paralysis.

docs.title: Production reality beats theoretical correctness
docs.text:
Many outages occur because teams trusted: - local tests - staging environments - architectural guarantees

over production signals.

Bedrock PHP treats production as the final arbiter, not an embarrassment to be explained away.

docs.title: Ownership prevents entropy
docs.text:
Unowned systems drift because: - maintenance is optional - upgrades are deferred - failures are someone else’s problem

Explicit ownership creates: - faster decisions - clearer accountability - safer deletions

docs.title: Deletion requires cultural permission
docs.text:
Teams often know something should be removed but delay because: - “what if we need it later?” - “someone might still be using it” - “it’s not broken”

By treating deletion as progress, Bedrock PHP gives permission to simplify.

Deletion reduces: - attack surface - operational burden - cognitive overhead

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Values erosion often shows up as:

  • exceptions justified by urgency
  • clever solutions without clear ownership
  • systems no one wants to touch
  • fear of removing anything

These are signals to re-anchor on the values.

Status: Draft — Chapter 3

docs.title: Normative
docs.text:
Declared defaults are binding assumptions that apply unless a project explicitly documents a deviation.

Defaults exist to eliminate silent disagreement.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: A Bedrock PHP system is a single PHP application running in a single primary runtime environment.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• One entry point for requests.
  • One runtime version per environment.
  • One deployment artifact.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No mixing multiple PHP runtimes in production.
  • No polyglot services inside the same system boundary.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If you feel the need for multiple runtimes, stop and evaluate whether this is still one system.

docs.title: Default 2: One Way to Do Things
Rule
docs.text: For any operational concern (deploying, configuring, running, debugging), there is exactly one supported way.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Documentation describes one path, not options.
  • Tooling supports the happy path only.
  • Deviations are visible and intentional.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No “advanced” or “alternative” paths hidden in tribal knowledge.
  • No parallel workflows that claim equivalence.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If two ways exist, remove one.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Non-production environments should behave like production in ways that matter, not in every detail.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Same PHP version and extensions.
  • Same configuration mechanisms.
  • Same deployment process.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No environment-specific code paths without documentation.
  • No skipping steps in non-production that matter in production.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If staging exists only to “check a box,” stop maintaining it or make it useful.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Behavior that matters operationally must be explicit and documented.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Configuration is declared, not inferred.
  • Startup fails fast on missing requirements.
  • Defaults are visible and reviewable.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No hidden behavior triggered by environment quirks.
  • No reliance on PHP.ini differences that are not tracked.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If someone cannot explain why the system behaves a certain way, make it explicit.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: When systems fail, replacement is preferred over in-place repair.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Instances are disposable.
  • Configuration and state are externalized.
  • Recovery procedures favor redeploying.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No manual production debugging as the primary recovery method.
  • No pets disguised as cattle.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If recovery requires SSH and hand edits, redesign the recovery path.

docs.title: Why declared defaults matter
docs.text:
Most production incidents are caused not by bad code, but by misaligned assumptions.

Declared defaults: - remove ambiguity - expose disagreement early - create shared expectations

Without defaults, every decision must be rediscovered under pressure.

docs.title: Single runtime keeps systems legible
docs.text:
Multiple runtimes introduce:

  • unclear ownership
  • uneven upgrade paths
  • inconsistent behavior

In PHP systems, this often shows up as: - mixed PHP versions - different extension sets - incompatible tooling

Keeping one runtime forces coherence.

docs.title: “One way” prevents folklore
docs.text:
When multiple workflows exist:

  • documentation lies
  • onboarding slows
  • incidents require the “right” person

A single path may be imperfect, but it is knowable.

If a second path is genuinely better, replace the first — don’t add it.

docs.title: Environment parity is about risk, not aesthetics
docs.text:
Perfect parity is impossible.

What matters: - deployment mechanics - configuration shape - runtime behavior

What usually does not matter: - instance size - traffic volume - exact infrastructure topology

Optimize parity where mistakes are expensive.

docs.title: Implicit behavior hides landmines
docs.text:
Implicit behavior:

  • surprises new team members
  • breaks silently during upgrades
  • is difficult to test

Common offenders in PHP: - php.ini drift - extension availability - error reporting differences

Making these explicit reduces future outages.

docs.title: Replace beats repair under pressure
docs.text:
Humans are bad at careful reasoning during incidents.

Replacement: - is repeatable - is faster - reduces variance

If the fastest way to recover is redeploying, you have designed well.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Defaults erode when:

  • teams say “just this once”
  • operational shortcuts become habits
  • new tools are added without removing old ones

If defaults are no longer true, update them or exit the standard.

Status: Draft — Chapter 4

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Deployment is a mechanical operation, not a creative act. If deploying requires judgment, the system is incomplete.

docs.title: Rule 1: Exactly One Production Deployment Path
Rule
docs.text: There is exactly one supported way to deploy to production.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• All production changes flow through the same pipeline.
  • The deployment path is documented and automated.
  • Access to deploy implies responsibility for production outcomes.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No manual production changes.
  • No SSH-based deploys.
  • No alternate pipelines “just for emergencies.”

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If two ways to deploy exist, remove one.

docs.title: Rule 2: Deployments Are Automated, Repeatable, and Reversible
Rule
docs.text: Every deployment must be: - automated end-to-end - reproducible from a clean checkout - reversible to the previous known-good state

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Builds produce immutable artifacts.
  • Rollbacks are faster than roll-forwards.
  • Deployment does not depend on local developer state.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No snowflake servers.
  • No mutable production instances.
  • No “hot fixes” applied directly to running systems.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If rollback is unclear or slow, stop shipping features and fix deployment.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: There is exactly one branch that deploys to production.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Production history is linear and auditable.
  • Releases are traceable to commits.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No ad-hoc production branches.
  • No force-pushing production history.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If you cannot identify what code is in production, stop deploying.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Use the smallest set of infrastructure components required to run the system.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Prefer managed services over self-hosted ones.
  • Avoid orchestration layers unless required by constraints.
  • Infrastructure complexity must earn its keep.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No service meshes, custom schedulers, or bespoke platforms by default.
  • No introducing infrastructure solely for future scale.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If infrastructure requires its own team to operate, reassess necessity.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text:
Each environment has a single, well-defined purpose.

  • dev: disposable, fast feedback
  • staging: production-like validation
  • production: the only user-facing truth

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Production is never debugged directly.
  • Staging mirrors production behavior, not scale.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No long-lived “almost production” environments.
  • No environment-specific deployment logic.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If an environment’s purpose is unclear, remove it.

docs.title: Why deployment must be boring
docs.text:
Deployments happen under stress:

  • late nights
  • incidents
  • deadlines

A boring deployment system: - reduces human error - enables faster recovery - builds confidence through repetition

Creativity belongs in product development, not release mechanics.

docs.title: The myth of the emergency deploy
docs.text:
Teams often justify alternate deploy paths by citing emergencies.

In practice: - emergencies are when humans are least reliable - alternate paths are least tested

The safest emergency deploy is the same one you always use, executed calmly.

docs.title: Immutable artifacts simplify everything
docs.text:
Immutable artifacts: - make rollbacks trivial - allow precise audits - decouple build from deploy

Common PHP examples: - versioned application packages - container images - read-only release directories

If you can change production without a new artifact, you have hidden risk.

docs.title: Minimal infrastructure ages better
docs.text:
Infrastructure accumulates cost:

  • maintenance
  • upgrades
  • knowledge

What felt cheap to add becomes expensive to keep.

Ask before adding infrastructure: - What problem does this solve today? - What breaks if we remove it?

If the answer is unclear, wait.

docs.title: Environment sprawl is a smell
docs.text:
Extra environments often exist to:

  • avoid deleting things
  • compensate for weak deployment confidence
  • satisfy vague safety feelings

Fewer environments with clear roles produce better outcomes.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Early signs of deployment drift:

  • “temporary” manual fixes
  • undocumented rollback steps
  • deployments requiring specific people
  • fear around releasing

These indicate the system is becoming fragile.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy deployment looks like: - a new engineer can deploy on day one - rollback is a button or single command - deploy frequency is limited by business choice, not fear

Unhealthy deployment looks like: - long runbooks for routine releases - hero engineers guarding production - downtime accepted as normal

Status: Draft — Chapter 5

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Configuration defines how the system behaves; state defines what the system remembers. Confusing the two creates fragile systems.

docs.title: Rule 1: Configuration Is External and Explicit
Rule
docs.text: All configuration is provided to the application externally and is explicit.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Configuration is environment-scoped.
  • Required configuration is validated at startup.
  • Configuration is reviewable alongside code changes.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No configuration hard-coded in application logic.
  • No configuration derived implicitly from environment quirks.
  • No “magic defaults” that materially alter behavior without visibility.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a behavior change cannot be explained by a configuration change, stop and make it explicit.

docs.title: Rule 2: Secrets Are Not Configuration Files
Rule
docs.text: Secrets are managed separately from non-secret configuration and are never committed to source control.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Secrets are injected at runtime via environment variables or a secrets provider.
  • Secret access follows least-privilege principles.
  • Secret rotation is planned and testable.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No secrets in repositories, images, or logs.
  • No shared credentials across environments.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a secret cannot be rotated without code changes, redesign secret handling.

docs.title: Rule 3: State Lives Outside the Application
Rule
docs.text: Application instances are stateless. All durable state lives in external systems.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Instances can be replaced at any time.
  • Horizontal or vertical scaling does not risk data loss.
  • Recovery procedures favor replacement over repair.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No writing durable data to local disk.
  • No relying on instance memory for correctness.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If losing an instance loses data, stop and externalize state.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Production instances are never modified in place to change behavior.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Configuration changes require redeployments.
  • Debugging does not involve live edits.
  • Drift between instances is impossible by design.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No SSH-based configuration edits.
  • No live patching of production instances.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If fixing an issue requires logging into a production box, redesign the workflow.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: The application must fail loudly and early when required configuration is missing or invalid.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Startup validation checks exist.
  • Errors are clear and actionable.
  • Misconfiguration does not produce partial or degraded behavior.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No silent fallbacks for required configuration.
  • No continuing startup in an unknown state.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If misconfiguration results in undefined behavior, stop and add validation.

docs.title: Configuration vs state (why the distinction matters)
docs.text:
Teams often blur configuration and state:

  • feature flags stored in databases
  • runtime decisions written to disk
  • behavior changes driven by mutable data

This makes systems hard to reason about and harder to recover.

Configuration answers: “How should the system behave?” State answers: “What has happened?”

Keep them separate.

docs.title: Why external configuration scales better
docs.text:
External configuration:

  • allows safe redeployments
  • supports immutable infrastructure
  • makes behavior reviewable

In PHP systems, this commonly means: - environment variables - managed configuration services - checked-in example config with validation

The mechanism matters less than visibility and consistency.

docs.title: Secrets deserve different handling
docs.text:
Secrets differ from configuration because:

  • exposure has real impact
  • rotation is inevitable
  • access should be minimized

Treating secrets like normal config often leads to: - accidental leaks - fear of rotation - brittle deploys

Design secret handling as a first-class concern.

docs.title: Statelessness enables confidence
docs.text:
Stateless applications:

  • are easier to scale
  • are easier to recover
  • are easier to reason about

If you are afraid to restart instances, the system owns you.

docs.title: Immutable instances prevent drift
docs.text:
Drift occurs when:

  • hotfixes are applied manually
  • config differs subtly across instances
  • debugging leaves residue

Immutability eliminates an entire class of bugs by design.

docs.title: Fail fast beats limp-along
docs.text:
Failing fast:

  • surfaces errors early
  • reduces blast radius
  • avoids corrupting state

A system that “mostly starts” is more dangerous than one that refuses to start.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Configuration drift often shows up as:

  • production-only bugs
  • fear of restarts
  • long-lived instances
  • undocumented environment variables

These are signals to reassert the rules.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy configuration looks like: - new environments spin up from documented config - missing config causes immediate failure - secrets can be rotated without deploy panic

Unhealthy configuration looks like: - copying env files by hand - mysterious production-only flags - secrets that no one wants to touch

Status: Draft — Chapter 6

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Observability exists to answer real operational questions under pressure, not to collect data for its own sake.

docs.title: Rule 1: Logging Is Mandatory and Actionable
Rule
docs.text: All production systems must emit logs that are usable during incidents.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Logs are centralized and searchable.
  • Logs include enough context to understand failures.
  • Log formats are consistent within the system.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No relying on local instance logs only.
  • No logs that require source-code archeology to interpret.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If logs cannot explain an incident after the fact, stop and improve logging.

docs.title: Rule 2: Errors Are Visible and Counted
Rule
docs.text: Unhandled errors and failures are surfaced automatically.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Exceptions are captured with stack traces.
  • Error rates are visible over time.
  • Alerts exist for sustained or severe failures.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No swallowing exceptions silently.
  • No relying on user reports as the primary error signal.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If users report errors before monitoring does, stop and fix visibility.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: The system exposes a small, intentional set of metrics tied to user impact.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Metrics answer: is it slow, is it broken, is it getting worse?
  • Metrics are stable over time.
  • Dashboards are simple and reviewed occasionally.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No metric explosion.
  • No dashboards without owners.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If metrics are not consulted during incidents, remove or replace them.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: It must be possible to correlate related events across logs and errors.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Request or correlation IDs exist where applicable.
  • Logs reference error identifiers.
  • Background jobs are traceable to triggering events when relevant.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No uncorrelated noise logs.
  • No tracing systems that are not understood by the team.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If correlation cannot be explained to a new team member, simplify it.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Observability data is retained long enough to investigate real incidents.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Retention policies are explicit.
  • Storage costs are understood and accepted.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No default retention without review.
  • No discarding data before it can reasonably be used.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If data is routinely unavailable during investigations, increase retention.

docs.title: Observability is not surveillance
docs.text:
Collecting everything:

  • increases cost
  • increases noise
  • slows down understanding

Good observability focuses on questions, not exhaust.

docs.title: Logs are the first responder
docs.text:
In most PHP systems:

  • logs are the fastest signal
  • metrics lag
  • traces are optional

Well-structured logs often solve incidents without anything else.

Log entries that help under pressure usually include: - timestamp - request or job identifier - error class and message - user or account identifier (when appropriate)

docs.title: Error visibility changes behavior
docs.text:
When errors are visible:

  • teams fix them earlier
  • failures feel real
  • quality improves naturally

When errors are hidden:

  • workarounds accumulate
  • incidents repeat
  • trust erodes

Visibility is a cultural lever.

docs.title: Metrics should map to user pain
docs.text:
Useful metrics often include:

  • request latency percentiles
  • HTTP 5xx rates
  • job failure counts

Metrics that look impressive but answer no question should be removed.

docs.title: Correlation reduces guesswork
docs.text:
Without correlation:

  • incidents become pattern-matching exercises
  • teams argue about causes

Correlation does not require full distributed tracing.

Often, a simple request ID propagated through logs is enough.

docs.title: Retention is a design decision
docs.text:
Too little retention: - hides slow-moving failures - prevents learning from incidents

Too much retention: - increases cost - encourages hoarding

Choose retention based on how long problems realistically surface.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Observability drift often appears as:

  • dashboards no one checks
  • alerts everyone ignores
  • logs no one trusts

These indicate observability theater, not observability.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy observability looks like: - incidents start with logs, not guesswork - alerts point to real problems - metrics guide prioritization

Unhealthy observability looks like: - silent failures discovered late - massive dashboards during outages - debates about what data means

Status: Draft — Chapter 7

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Performance work exists to meet defined targets, not to satisfy discomfort or speculation.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Performance targets must be defined before performance work begins.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Targets are measurable and time-bound.
  • Targets reflect user impact, not internal preference.
  • Targets are few.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No optimizing “because it feels slow.”
  • No tuning without baseline measurements.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If targets are being met in production, stop optimizing.

docs.title: Rule 2: Measure in Production or Production-Like Conditions
Rule
docs.text: Performance decisions are based on production data or realistic load tests.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Local benchmarks are advisory only.
  • Synthetic tests reflect real usage patterns.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No extrapolating from empty databases or idealized inputs.
  • No assuming linear scaling.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If measurements do not reflect reality, stop and redesign measurement.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Scale vertically before introducing horizontal scaling complexity.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Increase instance size first.
  • Simplify architecture before distributing it.
  • Delay coordination costs.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No sharding, queues, or distributed caches without necessity.
  • No premature load balancers.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If vertical scaling still meets targets, stop.

docs.title: Rule 4: Caching Is a Liability Until Proven Otherwise
Rule
docs.text: Introduce caching only when a measured bottleneck exists and invalidation is understood.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Cache scope is explicit.
  • Cache lifetime is deliberate.
  • Cache failure modes are known.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No caching “just in case.”
  • No caches without clear invalidation strategies.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If caching does not materially improve target metrics, remove it.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Scaling mechanisms must be removable without rewriting the system.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Scaling is additive, not foundational.
  • Core logic does not depend on scale artifacts.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No coupling correctness to scale.
  • No irreversible architectural commitments.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a scaling change cannot be rolled back, reconsider it.

docs.title: Why premature optimization hurts
docs.text:
Premature optimization:

  • hides real bottlenecks
  • increases maintenance cost
  • creates fragile complexity

Many performance issues disappear with: - better queries - faster disks - more memory

Before changing architecture, exhaust boring fixes.

docs.title: Production latency is what users feel
docs.text:
Users experience: - tail latency - timeouts - errors

Average performance often hides pain.

Focus on: - p95 / p99 latency - error rates under load

These metrics drive meaningful improvement.

docs.title: Vertical scaling buys time
docs.text:
Vertical scaling:

  • is fast to implement
  • is easy to reason about
  • delays distributed systems problems

Buying time is a valid strategy.

Use it to: - learn real usage - simplify code - prepare deliberate changes

docs.title: Caches fail in interesting ways
docs.text:
Caching introduces:

  • stale data
  • cache stampedes
  • coherence problems

In PHP systems, common cache pitfalls include: - assuming cache consistency - overloading shared caches - caching too close to business logic

Treat caches as optimization layers, not sources of truth.

docs.title: Reversibility protects you
docs.text:
Irreversible scaling decisions:

  • lock teams into complexity
  • make future changes expensive
  • turn temporary needs into permanent burden

Design scaling paths you can abandon.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Performance drift often shows up as:

  • architecture discussions without metrics
  • caching proposals before measurements
  • fear-driven scaling decisions

These are signals to slow down.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy performance work looks like: - targets agreed before changes - metrics guiding decisions - improvements validated in production

Unhealthy performance work looks like: - benchmarks without context - complex diagrams for simple systems - optimizations no one can explain later

Status: Draft — Chapter 8

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Security exists to reduce realistic risk over time, not to create the appearance of safety.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Every credential, role, and access path grants only the minimum permissions required.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Separate credentials per environment.
  • Narrowly scoped database users.
  • CI and automation credentials are limited and auditable.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No shared admin accounts.
  • No long-lived, over-privileged credentials.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a credential grants permissions "just in case," reduce it.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Dependencies, runtimes, and infrastructure are patched on a predictable cadence.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Patch and minor updates are routine maintenance.
  • Critical security updates are accelerated.
  • End-of-support timelines are tracked.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No deferring updates indefinitely.
  • No surprise major upgrades during incidents.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If patching feels heroic, simplify the system.

docs.title: Rule 3: Secrets Are Centralized and Rotatable
Rule
docs.text: Secrets are managed in a centralized system and can be rotated without code changes.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Secrets are never logged.
  • Access to secrets is monitored.
  • Rotation procedures are tested.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No secrets embedded in images or binaries.
  • No manual secret distribution.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a secret cannot be rotated safely, redesign its usage.

docs.title: Rule 4: Production Access Is Restricted and Audited
Rule
docs.text: Direct access to production systems is limited, time-bound, and auditable.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Access is granted for a reason and revoked when no longer needed.
  • Actions in production are attributable.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No standing, permanent production access for convenience.
  • No unaudited changes.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If access cannot be explained or justified, revoke it.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Security controls should be widely understood and operationally proven.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Use standard authentication and authorization mechanisms.
  • Prefer managed security features over bespoke ones.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• No custom crypto.
  • No homegrown auth unless required by constraints.

docs.title: Stop rule
docs.text: If a control requires a specialist to explain, reconsider it.

docs.title: Security is an operational discipline
docs.text:
Most real-world breaches occur due to:

  • forgotten credentials
  • unpatched systems
  • excessive permissions

Not due to missing advanced defenses.

Operational discipline prevents more incidents than novelty.

docs.title: Least privilege reduces blast radius
docs.text:
When something goes wrong:

  • narrow permissions limit damage
  • audit trails speed response

Over-privileged systems turn small mistakes into major incidents.

docs.title: Predictable patching builds trust
docs.text:
Teams that patch regularly:

  • fear updates less
  • recover faster
  • avoid forced upgrades

Security work should feel boring and routine.

docs.title: Secret rotation is a design test
docs.text:
If rotating a secret causes panic:

  • the system is too tightly coupled
  • access boundaries are unclear

Designing for rotation improves overall system hygiene.

docs.title: Production access is not a right
docs.text:
Permanent production access:

  • erodes discipline
  • hides risky behavior
  • complicates incident review

Temporary, audited access protects both the system and the team.

docs.title: Boring controls scale better
docs.text:
Well-known controls:

  • are documented
  • are supported
  • have known failure modes

Custom security solutions often become liabilities when the original authors leave.

docs.title: Things to watch for
docs.text:
Security drift often appears as:

  • credentials shared informally
  • upgrades postponed repeatedly
  • access granted "temporarily" and forgotten

These are early warning signs, not minor issues.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text:
Healthy security posture looks like: - routine dependency updates - short-lived credentials - clear access reviews

Unhealthy security posture looks like: - fear of touching secrets - ancient dependencies - unknown production access paths

Status: Draft — Chapter 9
